figures.caption: Fig. 1: Existing models struggle to differentiate between visual details in long-form and detailed captions. We propose caption ranking to capture fine-grained differences for video-language alignment.

Table 1: Comparison of our proposed Caption Degradation method to other on- and off-policy DPO data generation methods with the PLM model. Captions are evaluated by LLM-as-a-Judge along Relevance (Rel.), Descriptiveness (Descr.), Temporal Consistency (Temp C.), and Fluency (Flu.) on PE-Video.
Fig. 3: Examples from our Long-Form Multiple Choice Question Answering benchmark. Green represents the correct answer.

Fig. 5: Qualitative comparison of our Ranking model vs. an SFT baseline. Green highlights video-grounded descriptions. The Ranking model captures finer details better than SFT. See Appendix for more in-depth comparison and examples.

Fig. 2: Our proposed framework. Given a set of high-quality video captions, an LLM generates a totally ordered caption chain by repeatedly introducing visually-grounded errors into the previous caption. These caption chains are then used to train VLMs by having them rank the generated captions using ranking-based DPO. The captions’ similarity forces the models to pay attention to fine, visually-grounded details.
Fig. 4: Performance of Qwen2.5-VL and PLM w.r.t number of captions utilized in the chain. Model performance generally increases with chain length.
